title: The Towel Twist: Channeling Rage Mechanically
dek: When fury is trapped in the fascia, words are not enough. The Towel Twist is a mechanical, somatic practice to engage the body's resistance, unbind the jaw, and let the muscles speak the anger you were taught to swallow.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Body Holds the Story: Understanding Rage as Muscle Memory


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.076Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You wake in the early, blue-gray hours, the kind of cold that bites just beneath your skin. There’s a stubborn knot in the right trapezius, that dense muscle running from the base of your skull down the neck and across the shoulder blade. It’s not just tension. It’s the residue of anger - your anger - pressed tight into the fascia, that thin web of connective tissue that binds and shapes every part of you. The body knows this is not something to be shamed, hidden, or silenced. The trapezius remembers what your mind tries to forget.

When you were young, fury was the thing you learned to swallow, tuck away behind tight lips and clenched jaws. The masseter muscle, that powerhouse of the jaw, learned to clamp down, holding back words that might have been too loud, too sharp, too much. The psoas, deep in the belly, stiffens in anticipation, bracing against the storm you were told not to make. This is not pathology. This is biology.

The body is the first messenger of your rage, a messenger you can learn to listen to with fierce tenderness. To hear it, you start with the muscles, the fascia, the breath, the scent of sweat and earth rising from your skin. The Towel Twist is a mechanical channeling of that message, a somatic tool to engage the body’s own wisdom and release what has been held too long.

## A Mechanical Channel: Why the Towel Twist?

The Towel Twist is deceptively simple. It’s a way to use your body’s physical strength in a measured, intentional movement that does not seek to harm but to express and discharge. You grip a sturdy, rough towel - the kind that has soaked up steam from your kitchen sink or been steeped in herbal infusions - and you twist it with purpose. The fibers resist the torque, the muscles engage, and a pathway opens in the fascia.

This is not catharsis through destruction. It’s a reclamation of the power that your anger carries. When you twist the towel, you engage the flexors and extensors of your forearms, the pronators and supinators of your wrists, the biceps brachii and triceps brachii of your upper arms. Your shoulders mobilize, the levator scapulae lifts and lowers in a dance of release. The diaphragm, too, becomes a partner, rising and falling with the effort, the breath thickening with the exertion.

This mechanical action intersects with the biology of rage. Robert Augustus Masters, a somatic psychotherapist who works extensively with trauma and shame, reminds us that “the body is the first to know and the last to forget.” When you twist that towel, you’re not just moving muscles. You’re telling the body’s nervous system that it’s safe to express, safe to move the energy that has been trapped in freeze or fight. That stubborn knot in your trapezius begins to soften, the jaw unclenches, and the psoas feels invited to release its grip.

## The Jaw Remembers: Letting the Masseter Speak

You can feel it now, the masseter muscle - the thick muscle over your cheekbone that clamps your jaw shut when you’re holding back words you’re afraid to say. It’s midday, the sun is too bright through the window, and your breath is shallow, trapped behind the ribs by the tension in your face. You bring your hands to your jaw, fingers pressing gently along the bone, and you remind yourself that the jaw remembers.

Pema Chödrön’s teachings on radical acceptance come alive in this moment. She reminds us that “whatever you’re experiencing is teaching you.” Your jaw doesn’t hold anger because you failed. It holds anger because your body is trying to protect you, trying to keep you safe from the consequences of expression that might have been too costly in the past. When you twist the towel, the effort you give is transferred into a kind of permission for your jaw to soften. The masseter relaxes a little, the tightness becomes less of a clenched promise and more of a muscle ready to move.

You can add a herbal ally here - something like [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20), known in Traditional Chinese Medicine for its calming effects on the nervous system and its ability to relax muscle tension. Skullcap’s bitter, earthy aroma seeps into your skin when you rub a tincture along your temples and jawline, sending a signal to your brain that it’s okay to let go.

## The Breath and the Diaphragm: Expanding the Rage

As you twist, the diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle under your lungs, shifts. It’s the gatekeeper of your breath, the muscle that holds your capacity for both rage and release. When you’re angry but afraid, the diaphragm tightens, restricting the breath to shallow, quick gasps. The levator scapulae and trapezius stiffen in response, bracing your neck and shoulders for fight or flight.

Gabor Maté, who teaches extensively on trauma and the body’s response to stress, notes that “trauma is not what happens to you, but what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you.” In this framework, your body’s reaction to rage - tightened diaphragm, clenched jaw, stiff shoulders - is a map of past wounding. The Towel Twist invites your diaphragm to expand, to draw air fully into the belly, through the ribs, to the apex of the lungs.

You can feel the breath inflating the lungs, your psoas relaxing just enough to allow this expansion. The physical exertion creates a feedback loop: as muscles engage and release, the breath deepens, and with it, the nervous system shifts from a fight mode to a more regulated state. This is not a linear process. The rage might build, crest, and ebb several times during your practice.

## The Time of Night and the Smell of the Body

The best time to engage with the Towel Twist is often late at night, when the world is quiet, and the usual distractions vanish. It’s 2:13 a.m., and the house is steeped in silence save for the faint hum of the refrigerator. Your skin smells faintly of sweat and the herbal salve you rubbed on your neck earlier - a pungent blend of mugwort and lavender steeped in olive oil. This is not a scent meant to erase your rage but to honor its presence.

Mugwort, sacred in many herbal traditions, is known for its ability to stimulate dreams and open the subtle channels of the body. It is an ally for the night hours when rage can feel isolating and heavy. You notice the slight bitterness of the herb mingling with the salty musk of your own exertion as you twist the towel once more, the fibers creaking and resisting, just as your own body resists the full expression of what you feel.

The levator scapulae, which lifts the scapula and supports the neck, relaxes in small increments. You feel the release in the base of your skull, the area just behind the ear where tension often lodges. This physical sensation is both grounding and unsettling because it is unfamiliar territory: a place where rage feels embodied rather than judged.

## Resmaa Menakem and the Racialized Body of Rage

To understand the body’s relationship with rage in a cultural context, we turn to Resmaa Menakem, whose work centers on racialized trauma and the body. Menakem emphasizes that “the body keeps the score in the tissues of the body,” and that for many Black Americans, rage is a survival tool encoded in the muscles and fascia as protection against systemic violence.

When you engage in the Towel Twist, you are not only releasing personal anger but also tapping into a collective muscle memory that has long been denied. The act of twisting is a mechanical echo of the resilience encoded in the psoas and trapezius muscles, which hold the tension of generations. Menakem’s somatic approach insists that healing occurs in the body first, not in the mind, and that tools like the Towel Twist can be acts of defiance and restoration.

The physicality of the practice - gripping, twisting, feeling the resistance of the towel fibers - mirrors the hard work of unlearning internalized shame and reclaiming your right to rage. The body, in this framework, is not a neutral vessel but a battleground and a sanctuary.

## An Embodied Practice: The Towel Twist Ritual

You place a thick cotton towel on a sturdy surface - a table, a countertop, or even your lap. You fold it lengthwise so that it’s roughly six inches wide, thick enough to offer resistance but manageable in your hands. The room is dim, lit only by the flicker of a candle or the soft glow of a Himalayan salt lamp, the air tinged with the scent of dried rosemary and a drop of camphor essential oil.

You grip the towel at each end. Feel the tension in your fingertips, the subtle flex of your flexor digitorum muscles as you close your hands. Your forearms engage, the brachioradialis and pronator teres muscles rotating your wrists. You begin to twist the towel, slow at first, feeling the fibers resist. The effort grows as you twist harder, your trapezius muscles rising and your levator scapulae tightening briefly before the release.

Inhale deeply through your nose, drawing the breath down to the diaphragm. Feel your belly expand, your psoas muscle softening just enough to allow the breath to fill your lower ribs. Exhale through your mouth, slow and steady, the breath moving through the constricted pathways opened by your movement.

You repeat this cycle for several minutes, noticing the changes in your body - the slow unwinding of the masseter, the softening of the trapezius, the gentle release in the neck. You may feel a flush creeping up your neck, the heat of exertion mingling with the cool night air on your skin.

When you finish, you place the towel down gently, your palms tingling with the residual energy of the twist. You rest your hands over your heart, feeling the pulse beneath your fingers. The body has spoken, and you have listened.

## Herbal Allies: Medicine for the Muscles and Mind

Herbs are not mere additives to this practice. They are serious medicine, companions on the path of rage embodied. When your trapezius and levator scapulae muscles hold tight, a topical application of St. John’s Wort oil, steeped in olive oil and calendula, can soothe the fascia and ease inflammation. The herb’s deep, honeyed scent is a balm not only for the muscles but also for the nervous system, known for its mild antidepressant properties.

For internal support, you might turn to a tincture of Valerian root, a nervine that calms the nervous system and encourages restful sleep after a session of intense somatic work. Valerian’s earthy bitterness lingers on the tongue, reminding you that healing is never sweet or easy, but necessary.

Licorice root, used in Traditional Chinese Medicine to harmonize the qi, can be taken as a tea to support adrenal function when rage has become chronic stress. Its sweet, slightly woody aroma fills your cup as you sip, grounding you in the ritual of self-care.

These herbs, like the Towel Twist itself, do not erase your rage. They work with it, honor it, and support your body’s intelligence in moving through it.

## The Jaw, the Psoas, the Diaphragm: Rage’s Trifecta

The Towel Twist is most effective when you attend to the interconnectedness of the masseter, psoas, and diaphragm. The masseter clamps your jaw shut; the psoas braces your core; the diaphragm holds your breath captive. When these three muscles relax in sequence, the body experiences a profound shift.

Imagine the masseter unclenching first, a slow softening above the mandible. This allows the face to open, the eyes to soften. Next, the psoas releases, a deep letting go beneath the belly, where the muscle runs from the lumbar spine through the pelvis. This release is often felt as a warm, spreading sensation, as if a knot has been untied. Last, the diaphragm expands fully, drawing in breath that reaches every cell, inviting oxygen and life into the places rage has clenched tight.

This sequence is not a quick fix. It requires patience, presence, and permission to feel the discomfort that comes with unbinding old tensions. But it is the pathway back to your body’s natural state of flow and ease.

## Somatic Teachers on Rage and the Body

Thich Nhat Hanh, the Vietnamese Zen master, offered a profound teaching on the body as home for emotions. He invited practitioners to “smile to the body,” to recognize the body as the source of peace and the place where suffering is transformed. When you twist the towel, you can imagine offering a smile to your trapezius, to your masseter, to the psoas. This is an act of radical kindness to muscles that have been carrying burdens they were never meant to bear.

Bessel van der Kolk, whose pioneering work in trauma studies revolutionized our understanding of the body’s role in healing, emphasizes that “being able to feel safe with other people is probably the single most important aspect of mental health.” The Towel Twist, practiced in your own safe space, becomes a dialogue with your body, a way to tell the nervous system that it can relax, that it can let go.

Both teachers remind us that rage is not the enemy. Rage is a signal, a body-first messenger calling for attention, respect, and release.

## The Body Knows: Trusting Your Own Process

You may find that some nights, the Towel Twist ignites a firestorm of feelings - tears, trembling, heat flooding your skin. Other nights, it may feel like a gentle unspooling, a quiet loosening of long-held tension. Both are valid. The body’s wisdom is never hurried, never forced.

Your muscles will speak in their own language: the tightness in your levator scapulae, the pulse in your masseter, the flutter in your diaphragm. The scent of your skin, the taste of herbs on your tongue, the sound of your breath moving through your body - all these are signposts on your path.

You do not have to fix your rage. You only have to listen and move with it. The towel in your hands becomes more than fabric; it becomes a bridge between the mind’s fear and the body’s knowing.

---

## FAQ

**Q: Why use a towel? Can I use another object?**

**A:** The towel is ideal because of its flexible resistance. It allows you to engage multiple muscle groups without the risk of injury that comes from heavier equipment. Its texture provides sensory feedback, grounding your hands in the movement. You could experiment with similar objects - rope, resistance bands - but the towel’s tactile qualities are unique to this practice.

**Q: What if I feel dizziness or lightheadedness while twisting?**

**A:** Pay attention to your breath. Make sure you are breathing deeply and steadily, not holding your breath during the exertion. If dizziness occurs, pause, place your hands on your abdomen, and focus on slow, deep inhales and exhales. This signals your nervous system to calm and prevents hyperventilation.

**Q: How often should I practice the Towel Twist?**

**A:** There is no set schedule. Some find once a day helpful, especially during periods of high stress. Others use it as needed when rage surfaces. The key is to listen to your body and honor its rhythms.

**Q: Can this practice replace therapy or medical treatment?**

**A:** No. The Towel Twist is a somatic tool to complement professional care, not replace it. If you experience overwhelming rage or trauma symptoms, seek support from qualified mental health or medical providers.

**Q: Are there other herbs you recommend alongside this practice?**

**A:** Yes. Besides Skullcap and Valerian, consider passionflower for its calming properties or turmeric for systemic inflammation. Always consult a qualified herbalist or healthcare provider before starting new herbs, especially if you are on medication.

**Q: What if I feel embarrassed or ashamed about my rage?**

**A:** Shame is a heavy cloak, but remember, your body’s rage is not a moral failing. It’s a biological response to hurt and boundary violations. Teachers like Karla McLaren emphasize that emotions are messengers, not enemies. Allow yourself to witness your rage without judgment - it is part of your wholeness.

---

The Towel Twist is more than a movement. It is a reclaiming of your body’s voice, a mechanical channel for the fury you were taught to hide. When you twist, you untangle muscle and memory, inviting a new story to live in the sinew and skin. The body remembers. You do not have to forget.

---
